content:
Hi everyone,

I have been pondering this idea for a few days and would like to quickly check whether there is any reason not to do it.

Currently all our registers are defined into a single regs.rs file. There are textual separations to isolate groups, but otherwise they are all laid out flatly, which is a bit messy. Another unclean area is all the types we import from basically every module of the driver into regs.rs because register types need them. And the worst point: all registers are pub(crate), meaning any module can access any register, including those it has no business dealing with.

So how about splitting that file, and putting each register group under the module that directly uses them, with pub(super) visibility? For instance, all NV_PFALCON_* registers would be defined under falcon/regs.rs. No other module can touch falcon registers - if some functionality export is somehow needed, it would have to go through an interface of the falcon module.

I think this design would set clear ownership for every register and result in better interfaces overall (and less risk of poking at the wrong bits). Any argument against it?
This topic was moved here from #Nova > Moving register definitions into the module using them by Alexandre Courbot.
I also thought about this noticing that things are getting a bit messy, so I think that's a good idea.
Yeah I played a bit with it and couldn't see any reason why it wouldn't work
Maybe we can add this to the TODO list, or maybe @Nanonej wants to look into this?
Are you reading my mind or what :)

I agree that could be a good first issue. It's not particularly urgent, can be done in chunks, and while it's mostly moving code around it also requires understanding what is going on. @Nanonej, would you be interested?

Hello!

Yeah reading the RFC it sounds pretty simple, just moving code around, with the right visibility potentially making some interface. And looks like a good kaizen too for code maintenance and readability.

But, @Alexandre Courbot you made me realize that my setup couldn't allow me to contribute safely on Nova

It requires a recent NVIDIA GPU to test the code

I got a NVIDIA GPU on another setup, but it's a NVIDIA GEFORCE RTX 3080 FE, so I'm not sure if it's recent enough to test?

Your GPU (Ampere family) is definitely recent enough to be probed by Nova (because probing is all it can do for now :sweat_smile: )

Actually this task could also have been done without a GPU, because it's more of a if-it-builds-it-works kind of situation, but if you can test it that's of course even better.
And yes it will be a substantial improvement to the code's organization, and a meaningful contribution.
Ok, good then, I can check that with pleasure, looks like a good first contribution.
Don't hesitate to ask questions here, if any!
(deleted)

Hey! So after a coupled of days reading documentation to read how globally the Linux graphics stack is working, I'm ready to start.

As I can see, the modules which use those registers are: gsp/, fb, falcon/, gpu/.
So as I understand I should split the groups according which module is using them and not making a subfolder for each of those groups (e.g.: mc/regs.rs, bus/regs.rs, etc)

Also I see that some are used in different modules, like the NV_PGC6_* ones, should I then split this group in the different files? Would it make sense?

Yes, having a regs.rs within the corresponding submodule is the base idea. Ideally you shouldn't need to introduce new modules like mc, you can use the module that makes use of the register if there is only one (which should apply to most registers).

For registers used in several modules, the idea would be to add the register to the module it naturally belongs to, and then export the functionality needed by other modules through new methods of the module that owns the register (so each regs.rs can remain private to its module).

That second step requires a bit more design, so for now I'd suggest picking some registers that are used in only one module, and move them there as a first exercise - do the low-hanging fruits to get familiar before addressing the more tricky ones.

Ok, that make sense, I don't see quite well yet how to make that second step, and anyway as a "PoC" I planned to do one module first to see if I understood the way to do it right.

Thanks for the information, I'll try that then!
It's a baby step, but I just sent a first patch for review then.
I did a review (it looked mostly good!), have you seen it?

Hey @Alexandre Courbot, thanks a lot.
Yeah, but my life partner gave birth, since and I saw it from the maternity, but as you can guess I became really busy since :blush:
Hope I can continue the work later on, soon :fingers_crossed:

Oh, so it looks like the "baby step" is soon to become quite literal. :grinning_face_with_smiling_eyes:

Congratulations! It's fine to work on this at a slower pace - just please let us know if you think you will lack the time to do anything significant. It is very understandable that you have other priorities at the moment, and there will be other opportunities to contribute anyway (as a matter of fact I just posted another one).

Indeed, it looks like it :laughing: and thank you!

I think it should be ok to go back on it more actively soon, as the first month is almost over and that its the most tense between the discovery of baby care, the new daily life adaptation and the huge amount of paperwork :hot_face:

So I just took back your review just now to at least start to correct the patch.
Thanks again for the review, I'll try to send a v2 version in the week-end or next week :fingers_crossed: if as you suggest I can easily add another one or more extra obvious modules as additional patches.

Hello there!

So I made a new series of patch with the other "low hanging fruits" I found during my research and I use b4 as you suggested @Alexandre Courbot.

But then, I don't know if it's a bit messy to do a lot of little patch like b4 did, because it didn't squashed my commit compared to git format-patch. I wasn't sure, so I tried to make them nicely, with a correct description.
In case is too much let me know, I can squash them in the future :slight_smile:
I have yet to give it a deeper look, but that looks like the right granularity! Keeping things module-scoped is easier on review
Ok, that's what I was thinking, but I wasn't sure it would fit the email patch process as it feel a little bit more heavy that what I'm used to with Merge Requests :smile:

Hey!
Sorry, it have been a challenging past month so my follow-up patch series came late, but I just sent it.

I was wondering, I'm used to work using TDD, but I didn't see any test for Nova and even the Tests folder in gpu is quite empty.
So are tests welcome? And is there a recommended practice in Rust for Linux? As by default Rust write them in the same file but I saw in bigger project like Tokio, they use the integration test file to write unit test which looks tydier.
I also heard of KUnit but I guess it's something use in only for C and C++?
Please see https://docs.kernel.org/rust/testing.html
